items.correct: The value of the next-best alternative foregone when a choice is made — the real cost of a decision in terms of what you give up.
items.misconception: Opportunity cost is the total of everything you give up when making a choice.
items.random_wrong: Opportunity cost is the money price of something.
items.why: Opportunity cost is the NEXT BEST alternative, not the sum of all alternatives. If you can do A, B or C and choose A, the opportunity cost is whichever of B or C was your second choice — not both.

items.q: What does price elasticity of demand measure?
items.correct: The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price — the percentage change in quantity divided by the percentage change in price.
items.misconception: PED measures how flexible or stretchy a product is.
items.random_wrong: PED measures whether a product is expensive or cheap.
items.why: Students take 'elasticity' literally. It is a measure of sensitivity — how much quantity responds when price changes. The product itself isn't 'elastic'; demand for it is.

items.correct: A sustained rise in the general price level over time — reducing the purchasing power of money. Measured by the percentage change in a price index.
items.misconception: Inflation is when prices are high.
items.random_wrong: Inflation is when the economy grows too fast.
items.why: Inflation is about RISING prices, not high prices. A country can have stable high prices (no inflation) or rising low prices (high inflation). Students confuse level with rate of change.

items.q: If £1 rises from $1.20 to $1.40, the pound has?
items.why: It's Strengthened. “Weakened” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is a benefit of international trade?
items.correct: Wider consumer choice and lower prices
items.why: Correct answer: Wider consumer choice and lower prices. “Job losses in declining industries” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: £1 = $1.25. How many dollars do you get for £400?
items.why: Exact answer: 500. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
